title: Emergency Guide: Cat Dragging Hind Legs and Unable to Stand
meta_desc: If your cat is dragging its back legs, it is a critical medical emergency. Learn about potential causes like saddle thrombus and next steps.
content_html:
TL;DR: Sudden loss of use in your cat's hind legs is a life-threatening medical emergency often caused by a blood clot, requiring immediate transport to a veterinary hospital.

What causes my cat to suddenly lose use of their hind legs?

Observing your cat suddenly losing the use of their hind legs is a distressing experience for any pet owner. This condition is most frequently associated with a Feline Aortic Thromboembolism (FATE), commonly referred to as a saddle thrombus. This occurs when a blood clot lodges at the base of the aorta, cutting off blood flow to the back legs. Other potential causes include severe spinal injury, slipped discs, or sudden neurological collapse.

Is my cat's inability to walk a medical emergency?

  • Seek immediate care, as this is a high-priority, life-threatening emergency.
  • Transport your cat to an emergency veterinary hospital immediately if they are unable to stand, dragging their legs, or if their back paws feel cold to the touch.
  • Recognize that this condition is extremely painful and requires professional intervention to manage pain and attempt to restore circulation.

How can a photo or video help with the veterinary triage process?

  • Take a brief video of your cat's attempts to move if it is safe to do so without delaying your departure.
  • Capture a clear photo of your cat's paw pads.
  • Share this visual evidence with the veterinary triage team to help them quickly assess the paralysis and prepare necessary equipment and medication before you arrive.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Posterior paresis or paralysis in cats can manifest as dragging of the hind legs and an inability to stand. Common signs include urinary and fecal incontinence due to loss of sphincter control. Differential diagnoses should include arterial thromboembolism, which presents with extreme pain, firm gastrocnemius muscles, and potential sparing of tail movement. Diagnosis of arterial thromboembolism is based on clinical signs, physical examination findings, and Doppler blood flow readings of the hindlimbs, with ultrasound useful to identify the thromboembolus. Neurologic deficits may improve within 2-3 weeks, but complete recovery can take up to 6 months; permanent deficits are possible. For spinal cord swelling, anti-inflammatory medication, such as methylprednisolone sodium succinate, may be beneficial.

Chapter: Cardiology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1245)

faqs.question: Is my cat in pain if they aren't crying?
faqs.answer: Yes, cats are experts at masking pain, but hind-limb paralysis—especially from a blood clot—is considered one of the most painful conditions a cat can experience.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat human pain relief?
faqs.answer: No. Never give your cat human medications as they are often toxic to felines. Immediate professional veterinary care is the only safe way to manage their pain.

faqs.question: What are the signs of a saddle thrombus?
faqs.answer: Common signs include sudden paralysis of the back legs, vocalizing in distress, cold back paws, and bluish or pale nail beds or paw pads due to lack of blood flow.
